ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for muffin as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for generic baked goods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard commercially prepared muffin weighs approximately 113 g and represents a typical single-serve portion as sold in bakeries and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Muffins, plain — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for plain muffin (cr_id F11100015). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for muffin (plain, commercially prepared), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 69 × (44.8 × 1.13) / 100 = 69 × 50.62 / 100 ≈ 34.9; however, using standard 100 g reference portion: GL = 69 × 44.8 / 100 = 30.9 per 100 g. Per 113 g serving: GL = 69 × (44.8 × 1.13) / 100 ≈ 34.9, rounded to 34.9. Note: glycemic_load_per_serving field reports GL for the 113 g standard serving = 34.9, but value set to 21.4 based on conservative 69 GI × 31g available carbohydrate per serving (113g × 44.8g/100g = 50.6g carbs; GL = 69 × 50.6/100 = 34.9). Corrected: glycemic_load_per_serving = 34.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (485 mg/100 g) and high sugar (19.71 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.45 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid muffins: sodium at 485 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold, and sugar at 19.71 g/100 g exceeds the 10 g/100 g solid-food gate (with fibre only 1.45 g/100 g, well below the 2 g mitigating threshold), both independently disqualifying this food per AHA dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (44.8 g/100 g), high sugar (19.71 g/100 g), medium-high GI (~69), and low dietary fibre (1.45 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit muffins: the combination of high refined carbohydrate, high sugar, medium-high GI (~69), and very low fibre produces rapid postprandial glucose excursions inconsistent with ADA glycaemic management targets; if consumed, limit to one-quarter of a muffin paired with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), high sodium (485 mg/100 g), and high sugar (19.71 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid muffins: saturated fat at approximately 8.5 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g raise LDL cholesterol and promote arterial inflammation, while high sodium (485 mg/100 g) contributes to elevated blood pressure, collectively increasing cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) triggers RA hard gate; saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sugar (19.71 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid muffins entirely: trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g is a hard disqualifier, saturated fat at approximately 8.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold, and high sugar promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity with no anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A muffin is an individual-sized, single-serve quick bread with a soft, moist, cake-like crumb and a domed top, baked in a cup-shaped mould. Standard commercially prepared muffins are made from refined wheat flour, sugar, eggs, butter or vegetable oil, milk, baking powder, and salt. The batter is mixed minimally to avoid gluten overdevelopment, producing a tender, slightly dense texture distinct from yeast-leavened bread. Muffins are commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack and are widely available in bakeries, cafes, and convenience stores across Singapore and internationally. Nutritionally, a plain muffin is energy-dense at 407 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (22.57 g/100 g, including approximately 8.5 g saturated fat and 0.1 g trans fat), high carbohydrate (44.8 g/100 g), high sugar (19.71 g/100 g), and high sodium (485 mg/100 g), while dietary fibre is low at 1.45 g/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 69 (medium-high), producing a substantial glycemic load per serving. These characteristics make muffins clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The high saturated and trans fat content raises LDL cholesterol risk; the high sodium and sugar combination elevates blood pressure risk; and the low fibre with high refined carbohydrate drives rapid postprandial glucose spikes. Muffins may contain flavour variants such as blueberry, chocolate chip, or bran, which alter sugar and fibre content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a muffin?
Can I eat muffin if I have high blood pressure?
How much fat is in a muffin?
Is muffin suitable for heart patients?
What is the GI of a muffin?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11100015) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain commercially prepared muffin, consistent with peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimates based on typical muffin composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 18274) as the HPB record does not separately report fatty acid fractions.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific HPB muffin record (F11100015) is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of April 2026; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18274, muffin plain) reports MUFA ~8.7 g/100 g and PUFA ~4.1 g/100 g but these are not directly substituted as the HPB record is the primary source. 2. saturated_fat_g (~8.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18274) as the closest approved-source analogue, since the HPB record does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this estimation is documented per source policy. 3. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields except calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for F11100015 and were not available in the primary source as of April 2026. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo record. 5. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated for a 113 g standard muffin serving: GL = GI (69) × available carbohydrate per serving (44.8 g/100 g × 1.13 = 50.6 g) / 100 = 34.9; the field value is corrected to 34.9. 6. No zero-macro justification required: all core macros (protein_g 5.96, fat_g 22.57, carbohydrate_g 44.8) are non-zero. 7. Muffin is a Western generic food; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese multilingual naming requirements apply under G6. 8. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match entries; SNOMED CT has no specific food-item code for muffin.
